← All posts February 19, 2026

Finding a prime factor of a number you'll never compute

  • math
  • putnam
  • number-theory

This week’s problem was a Putnam classic (2015, A2):

Let a0=1a_0 = 1a0​=1, a1=2a_1 = 2a1​=2, and an=4an−1−an−2a_n = 4a_{n-1} - a_{n-2}an​=4an−1​−an−2​ for n≥2n \ge 2n≥2. Find an odd prime factor of a2015a_{2015}a2015​.

The number a2015a_{2015}a2015​ is astronomically large — the sequence grows like (2+3)n(2+\sqrt{3})^n(2+3​)n — so the only hope is structure, not computation.

Closed form. The characteristic equation y2−4y+1=0y^2 - 4y + 1 = 0y2−4y+1=0 has roots

α=2+3,β=2−3,\alpha = 2 + \sqrt{3}, \qquad \beta = 2 - \sqrt{3},α=2+3​,β=2−3​,

and matching initial conditions gives

an=αn+βn2.a_n = \frac{\alpha^n + \beta^n}{2}.an​=2αn+βn​.

A nice bonus, using αβ=1\alpha\beta = 1αβ=1: extending the recurrence backwards gives a−1=4a0−a1=2=a1a_{-1} = 4a_0 - a_1 = 2 = a_1a−1​=4a0​−a1​=2=a1​, and in general a−n=ana_{-n} = a_na−n​=an​ — the sequence is symmetric around 0.

The key divisibility. For odd kkk, the sum αkn+βkn\alpha^{kn} + \beta^{kn}αkn+βkn factors over αn+βn\alpha^n + \beta^nαn+βn (it’s the odd-exponent sum-of-powers factorization):

αkn+βknαn+βn=α(k−1)n−α(k−2)nβn+⋯+β(k−1)n,\frac{\alpha^{kn} + \beta^{kn}}{\alpha^n + \beta^n} = \alpha^{(k-1)n} - \alpha^{(k-2)n}\beta^{n} + \cdots + \beta^{(k-1)n},αn+βnαkn+βkn​=α(k−1)n−α(k−2)nβn+⋯+β(k−1)n,

and using αβ=1\alpha\beta = 1αβ=1 this quotient is a symmetric integer expression. So for odd kkk,

an∣akn.a_n \mid a_{kn}.an​∣akn​.

Cashing it in. Since 2015=5×4032015 = 5 \times 4032015=5×403 and 403403403 is odd, take n=5n = 5n=5, k=403k = 403k=403:

a5∣a2015.a_5 \mid a_{2015}.a5​∣a2015​.

So any prime factor of a5a_5a5​ is automatically a prime factor of a2015a_{2015}a2015​, and a5a_5a5​ is small enough to just compute:

a2=4(2)−1=7,a3=4(7)−2=26,a4=4(26)−7=97,a5=4(97)−26=362.a_2 = 4(2) - 1 = 7,\quad a_3 = 4(7) - 2 = 26,\quad a_4 = 4(26) - 7 = 97,\quad a_5 = 4(97) - 26 = 362.a2​=4(2)−1=7,a3​=4(7)−2=26,a4​=4(26)−7=97,a5​=4(97)−26=362.

Factor it: 362=2×181362 = 2 \times 181362=2×181, and 181181181 is prime. Therefore 181 divides a2015a_{2015}a2015​.

The move that makes this problem is refusing to engage with a2015a_{2015}a2015​ directly. The divisibility relation an∣akna_n \mid a_{kn}an​∣akn​ is a lever: it converts a question about the 2015th term into a question about the 5th, and from there it’s arithmetic you can do in the margin.
